perturbation.original_text: 5.1 The Professional shall at all times during the Term refrain from: 5.1.1 dishonest, fraudulent, illegal or unethical acts or omissions; 5.1.2 excessive use or abuse of alcohol; 5.1.3 use of controlled substances, except as prescribed by a licensed medical professional in the treatment of illness or disease; 5.1.4 acts or omissions reasonably determined by the Company to be prejudicial or injurious to the business or goodwill of the Company, its officers, employees, shareholders or products, the golf industry or professional golf; and 5.1.5 conduct which could reasonably be expected to degrade the Professional, devalue the services of the Professional or to bring the Professional into public hatred, contempt, scorn or ridicule, or that could reasonably be expected to shock, insult or offend the community or to offend public morals or decency.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 The Professional should generally refrain from: 5.1.1 dishonest or fraudulent acts or omissions; 5.1.2 frequent consumption of alcohol; 5.1.3 using controlled substances to a concerning degree, except as prescribed by a licensed medical professional; 5.1.4 acts or omissions perceived by the Company to be somewhat prejudicial to the business or goodwill of the Company, its officers, employees, shareholders or products, the golf industry or professional golf; and 5.1.5 conduct which could potentially degrade the Professional or devalue the services of the Professional.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict obligation on the Professional to refrain from certain behaviors. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with 'should generally,' and using vague terms like 'frequent consumption,' 'concerning degree,' 'perceived by the Company to be somewhat prejudicial,' and 'potentially degrade.' This reduces the enforceability of the clause and makes it harder to determine when a breach has occurred. The Professional is based in New Mexico.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Mexico Uniform Commercial Code - Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's vagueness introduces an element of potential bad faith in the enforcement of the behavioral clauses. The original text's clear prohibitions are replaced with ambiguous guidelines, allowing the Company to potentially interpret the Professional's actions subjectively. This subjectivity could be seen as a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under the New Mexico Uniform Commercial Code (NMSA 1978, § 55-1-304), which requires honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing in the performance and enforcement of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: New Mexico Statutes Section 55-1-304 (2018) - Obligation of good faith. :: 2018 New Mexico Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia Go to Other Versions of The N.M. Stat. 2024 N.M. Stat. 2023 N.M. Stat. 2021 N.M. Stat. 2018 N.M. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2018 New Mexico Statutes Chapter 55 - Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 - General Provisions Section 55-1-304 - Obligation of good faith. Universal Citation: NM Stat § 55-1-304 (2018) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 55-1-304. Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code [ 55-1-101 NMSA 1978] imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History: 1953 Comp., § 50A-1-203, enacted by Laws 1961, ch. 96, § 1-203 1978 Comp. §55-1-205; recompiled by compiler as 1978 Comp. § 55-1-303; Laws 2005, ch. 144, § 18 . ANNOTATIONS OFFICIAL COMMENTS UCC Official Comments by ALI & the NCCUSL. Reproduced with permission of the PEB for the UCC. All rights reserved. Source. — Former Section 1-203 [ 55-1-203 NMSA 1978]. Changes from former law. — Except for changing the form of reference to the Uniform Commercial Code, this section is identical to former Section 1-203. 1. This section sets forth a basic principle running throughout the Uniform Commercial Code. The principle is that in commercial transactions good faith is required in the performance and enforcement of all agreements or duties. While this duty is explicitly stated in some provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the applicability of the duty is broader than merely these situations and applies generally, as stated in this section, to the performance or enforcement of every contract or duty within this Act. It is further implemented by Section 1-303 [ 55-1-303 NMSA 1978] on course of dealing, course of performance, and usage of trade. This section does not support an independent cause of action for failure to perform or enforce in good faith. Rather, this section means that a failure to perform or enforce, in good faith, a specific duty or obligation under the contract, constitutes a breach of that contract or makes unavailable, under the particular circumstances, a remedial right or power. This distinction makes it clear that the doctrine of good faith merely directs a court towards interpreting contracts within the commercial context in which they are created, performed, and enforced, and does not create a separate duty of fairness and reasonableness which can be independently breached. 2. "Performance and enforcement" of contracts and duties within the Uniform Commercial Code in
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the original obligations. This undermines the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The law explanation argues that the subjective interpretation allowed by the vagueness could be seen as a violation, and NMSA 1978, § 55-1-304, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The change from 'shall' to 'should generally' and the use of terms like 'frequent consumption' and 'potentially degrade' create ambiguity, making the enforcement of the behavioral clauses questionable and potentially violating the obligation of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 6.4 The Professional agrees to maintain at all times during the Term such insurance, including without limitation, health insurance, workers' compensation, automobile and general comprehensive liability coverage, as will protect and hold harmless the Company from any claims, losses, damages, costs, expenses or liability arising out of the Services performed under this Agreement. The Company may require the Professional to provide insurance certificates evidencing the same.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.4 The Professional should endeavor to maintain some form of insurance coverage during the Term, potentially including health insurance, workers' compensation, automobile and general comprehensive liability coverage, as may be deemed reasonably necessary to protect and hold harmless the Company from certain claims arising out of the Services performed under this Agreement. The Company may, from time to time, request the Professional to provide some form of documentation regarding her insurance status.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly mandates the Professional to maintain specific insurance coverages. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'agrees to maintain' with 'should endeavor to maintain some form of insurance coverage,' making the insurance requirement discretionary. The phrases 'as may be deemed reasonably necessary' and 'certain claims' introduce further ambiguity. The shift from 'insurance certificates' to 'some form of documentation' also weakens the requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's ambiguity regarding workers' compensation insurance potentially violates New Jersey workers' compensation laws, specifically N.J.S.A. 34:15-79. The ambiguity around if the Professional needs workers' compensation insurance coverage when the Company is based out of New Jersey introduces a scenario where the Professional may not obtain workers' compensation coverage due to the ambiguous nature of the contract, leaving the Company liable for any potential claims.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2013 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 34 - LABOR AND WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION Section 34:15-79 - Penalties for failure to carry insurance. NJ Rev Stat § 34:15-79 (2013) What's This? 34:15-79 Penalties for failure to carry insurance. 34:15-79. a. An employer who fails to provide the protection prescribed in this article, who misrepresents one or more employees as independent contractors, or who provides false, incomplete or misleading information concerning the number of employees , shall be guilty of a disorderly persons offense and, if the failure, misrepresentation or provision of false, incomplete or misleading information is knowing, shall be guilty of a crime of the fourth degree, and shall be subject to a stop-work order issued by the Director of the Division of Workers' Compensation pursuant to subsection e. of this section. In cases where a workers' compensation award in the Division of Workers' Compensation of New Jersey against the defendant is not paid at the time of the sentence, the court may suspend sentence upon that defendant and place him on probation for any period with an order to pay the delinquent compensation award to the claimant through the probation office of the county. Where the employer is a corporation, any officer who is actively engaged in the corporate business, including, but not limited to, the president, vice-president, secretary, and the treasurer thereof shall be liable for failure to secure the protection prescribed by this article. Any contractor placing work with a subcontractor shall, in the event of the subcontractor's failing to carry workers' compensation insurance as required by this article, become
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's requirement for the Professional to maintain insurance, potentially leading to a violation of N.J.S.A. 34:15-79, which mandates employers to provide workers' compensation insurance. The original text states the 'Professional agrees to maintain... insurance,' while the modified text uses the phrase 'should endeavor to maintain some form of insurance coverage,' making the insurance requirement discretionary. The scraped law snippet 1 (Section 34:15-79) outlines penalties for employers who fail to provide the prescribed worker's compensation. The modified text's ambiguity could result in the Professional not obtaining necessary workers' compensation coverage, thus potentially leaving the Company liable and non-compliant with New Jersey law. The shift from a mandatory 'agrees to maintain' to a discretionary 'should endeavor to maintain' directly contradicts the legal obligation implied by the law to provide such protections, especially for worker's compensation.
